---
title: "Getting Started"
subtitle: "Installing your tools and setting up the template"
---

## Overview

Before you can build or edit your website, you need to set up a few free tools on your
computer. This chapter walks you through every installation step and gets the website
template running on your machine for the first time.

By the end of this chapter, you will have:

- R installed on your computer
- RStudio installed and open
- Quarto installed
- The website template downloaded and running in your browser

::: {.callout-note}
These installations only need to be done once. Once your environment is set up, you won't
need to repeat these steps.
:::

---

## Step 1: Install R

**R** is a free programming language that powers the tools behind your website. Even if
you never write R code yourself, it needs to be installed for Quarto and RStudio to work
properly.

1. Go to the official R download page: [https://cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org)
2. Under *"Download and Install R"*, click the link that matches your operating system:
   - **Windows** → click *"Download R for Windows"* → then *"base"* → then the download link at the top
   - **Mac** → click *"Download R for macOS"* → choose the version that matches your Mac
   - **Linux** → select your distribution and follow the instructions
3. Open the downloaded file and follow the installer prompts. The default settings are
   fine — you don't need to change anything.

::: {.callout-tip}
**Not sure which version to download?** If you're on a newer Mac (2020 or later), you
likely have an Apple Silicon chip — look for the file labeled **arm64**. If you're on an
older Mac or a Windows PC, the standard download is the right choice.
:::

---

## Step 2: Install RStudio

**RStudio** is a free application that gives you a visual, beginner-friendly environment
for working with R and Quarto. Think of it as the workspace where everything comes
together.

::: {.callout-important}
You must install R **before** installing RStudio. RStudio requires R to be present on
your computer in order to run.
:::

1. Go to the RStudio download page:
   [https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop)
2. Click **"Download RStudio Desktop"** — the free version is all you need.
3. Choose the installer for your operating system, download it, and run it.
4. Follow the installer prompts. Again, the default settings are fine.
5. Once installed, open RStudio. You should see a window divided into several panels.

::: {.callout-tip}
**What am I looking at?** When RStudio opens for the first time, you'll see a few panels:
the **Console** (where R runs commands), the **Environment** (where your data and
variables live), and the **Files** panel (where you browse your project files). Don't
worry about mastering these right away — you'll get familiar with them as you go.
:::

---

## Step 3: Install Quarto

**Quarto** is the tool that actually builds your website. It reads your content files and
turns them into the web pages visitors will see. You'll install it directly from inside
RStudio using the R Console — no need to visit any external websites or run a separate
installer.

::: {.callout-note}
The **Console** is the tab in the bottom-left panel of RStudio where you type and run R
commands. It usually has a `>` symbol waiting for your input. This is different from the
**Terminal** tab next to it — the Console is for R code, while the Terminal is for
system-level commands.
:::

1. Open **RStudio**
2. Click on the **Console** tab in the bottom-left panel
3. Click next to the `>` symbol and type the following command, then press **Enter**:
```r
install.packages("quarto")
```

4. R will download and install the Quarto package. You may see a lot of text scroll by —
   that's normal. Wait until you see the `>` prompt appear again, which means it's done.
5. Once installed, load the package by typing the following and pressing **Enter**:
```r
library(quarto)
```

6. To confirm everything installed correctly, run:
```r
quarto_path()
```

If a file path is printed in the Console (for example, `/usr/local/bin/quarto`), Quarto
is installed and ready to go.

::: {.callout-tip}
**Seeing a warning that says the package was built under a different version of R?**
That's usually fine and won't affect anything in this tutorial. As long as the `>` prompt
returns without an error message in red, you're good to continue.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
If you see a red error message saying Quarto was not found or the path is empty, it may
mean Quarto's command-line tools are not installed on your system yet. In that case,
visit [https://quarto.org/docs/get-started](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started), download
the installer for your operating system, and run it. Then return to this step and run
`quarto_path()` again to confirm.
:::

---

## Step 4: Create a Free GitHub Account

**GitHub** is a free platform where people store and share code and projects. You'll use
it to download the website template and later to publish your site online.

If you already have a GitHub account, you can skip to Step 5.

1. Go to [https://github.com](https://github.com) and click **"Sign up"**
2. Follow the prompts to create a free account.
3. Verify your email address when prompted.

::: {.callout-tip}
Choose a professional username if you can — your GitHub profile may be visible to
collaborators or employers in the future.
:::

---

## Step 5: Download the Website Template

You'll download the template directly from GitHub as a ZIP file — no special tools or
commands needed for this step, just your web browser.

1. Go to the template repository in your browser:
   [https://github.com/vmahan1998/Personal_Website_Template](https://github.com/vmahan1998/Personal_Website_Template)
2. Click the green **"Code"** button near the top-right of the page
3. In the dropdown that appears, click **"Download ZIP"**
4. Your browser will download a file called `Personal_Website_Template-main.zip`
5. Once downloaded, **unzip the file**:
   - **Windows:** Right-click the ZIP file → select *"Extract All"* → choose a destination
     (Desktop or Documents is fine) → click *"Extract"*
   - **Mac:** Double-click the ZIP file — it will automatically unzip into a folder in the
     same location
6. You should now have a folder called `Personal_Website_Template-main` containing all
   the website files

::: {.callout-tip}
Move the unzipped folder somewhere easy to find and remember — you'll be opening and
editing files inside it throughout this tutorial. Avoid leaving it in your Downloads
folder where it can get buried.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
**What's inside the folder?** Don't worry if the contents look unfamiliar right now.
The next chapter will walk you through what each file and folder does. For now, you just
need the folder in place and ready to open.
:::

---

## Step 6: Open the Project in RStudio

Now you'll open the template as a **project** in RStudio. Opening it as a project (rather
than just opening individual files) is important — it tells RStudio where all your files
live so that everything works correctly together.

1. Open **RStudio**
2. Go to **File → Open Project**
3. Navigate to the `Personal_Website_Template-main` folder you just unzipped
4. Inside the folder, find the file ending in **`.Rproj`** and click on it
5. Click **"Open"**

RStudio will reload and you'll see all the template files appear in the **Files** panel
in the bottom-right corner of the screen.

::: {.callout-note}
The `.Rproj` file is what tells RStudio to treat this folder as a self-contained project.
It sets up your working environment automatically so all the files and folder paths
work correctly without any extra configuration on your part.
:::
